prompt:
Research query: How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?

Below are evidence statements from a literature review. Extract ALL named studies, trials, and author references mentioned.

EVIDENCE:
- During damp heat aging, higher temperature increases both the chemical reaction rate and the diffusion coefficient, promoting more water diffusion into the adhesive and further causing aging and degradation of the joint.
- Effects of damp heat aging on fiber-reinforced composites include reversible effects mainly from moisture and irreversible effects such as reduction of interface strength, appearance of microstructure defects, and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix.
- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased by approximately 31.3% after moisture absorption and by 54.4% under combined high temperature (95 C) and humid environment.
- PV industry consensus holds that PEVA encapsulant should have vinyl acetate content between 28 and 33%, but PEVA with lower VA content of 18 and 24% showed greater tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking after damp heat aging.
- PEVA with higher VA content (33 and 40%) degraded significantly after 2000 hours of damp heat aging with significant loss in transmittance and mechanical integrity.
- The study recommends reducing VA content in PEVA to 24% for better damp heat aging resistance since PEVA18 has inadequate transmittance and relatively high stiffness after 2000 h of DH aging.
- Thermal aging by 5,000 thermocycles induced a significant decrease in micro-tensile bond strength values for all seven adhesive systems tested, with one-step self-etch adhesives showing the lowest results.
- After 24-hour water storage at 37 C, total-etch adhesives XP Bond and Adper Scotchbond 1 XT showed highest MTBS values, while one-step self-etch adhesives Bond Force, Xeno V, and G-Bond were lowest.
- Three-way ANOVA showed that both substrate type and aging method had statistically significant effects on bond strength (p=0.00), but the adhesive approach (self-etch vs etch-and-rinse) did not affect bond strength (p=0.84).
- Butyl rubber edge seal has two orders of magnitude lower water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) compared with UV-curable epoxies and polymeric encapsulants, and a 1.5 cm bond width is modeled adequate for 25-year lifetime.
- Polyolefin encapsulants showed no chemical reaction with perovskite, high electrical resistivity reducing PID susceptibility, and low elastic modulus enabling thermal cycle survival between -40 C and 85 C.
- Encapsulant elastic modulus comparison showed EVA at 10 MPa, Surlyn at 394 MPa, and polyolefin variants from 7 to 20 MPa, with lower modulus polyolefins showing best damp heat survival without delamination.
- XPS detected carboxylic acid groups on fractured encapsulant surfaces and deprotonated silanol and water bands on glass sides, indicating hydrolytic degradation at the encapsulant-glass interface.
- Compressive shear performance of EVA double-glass laminates was not significantly affected by damp heat aging at 85 C/85% RH for up to 10,000 hours, while POE laminates showed even improved compressive shear performance.
- EVA laminates consistently failed interfacially regardless of DH duration, while POE laminates transitioned from purely interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive failure after 10,000 hours.
- Hygrothermal (damp heat) aging treatment at 50 C and 98% humidity caused bending strength reductions of 16-51% in polymer-rich anchoring adhesives depending on adhesive composition, with sampling periods up to 3000 hours.
- Relative maintained peel force at the glass-encapsulant interface after 3000 hours of damp heat at 85 C/85% RH varied from 15.7% to 73.0% depending on encapsulant material and lamination process.
- EVA laminates showed less peel force degradation after damp heat than POE samples, and higher lamination temperature was beneficial for peel force and gel content for cross-linking encapsulants.
- TPO with PET/Al/PET/Olefin backsheet had peel forces around 160 N/cm before damp heat but dropped to 33-45 N/cm after 3000 hours DH, the most severe absolute degradation observed.
- Activation energy for adhesion degradation between backsheet and encapsulant under damp heat was approximately 51 kJ/mol from Arrhenius analysis at 85% RH with temperatures of 95, 85, and 65 C.
- Rate of adhesion degradation is proportional to moisture concentration at the interface with Arrhenius temperature dependence, and temperature acceleration is faster than relative humidity acceleration.
- Adhesion strength follows exponential decay model S = S0*exp(-t/t_char)^beta where degradation slope parameters depend on temperature and humidity stress levels.
- PV modules laminated at elevated temperatures up to 180 degrees C withstood DH3000 damp heat aging tests for peel force and yellowing.
- There exists a crosslinking saturation level above which gel content influence on adhesion becomes negligible.
- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion in mini-modules falls to a level generally seen after more than 15 years of field exposure.
- Dentin bond strengths of one-application adhesive systems decreased with increasing relative humidity across six environmental conditions.
- POE encapsulant coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability under thermal aging, followed by EPE then EVA.
- Adhesion testing on coupon-level EVA, POE, and EPE specimens showed decreases in adhesion energy Gc from 5000 hours of hot-dry (90 degrees C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90 degrees C, 60% RH) aging.
- FTIR-ATR showed insignificant bulk encapsulant degradation under thermal aging alone, demonstrating the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating degradation.
- Adhesion energy Gc decreased for EVA, POE, and EPE encapsulants after 5000 hours of both hot-dry (90 degrees C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90 degrees C, 60% RH) aging.
- Thermal degradation of the encapsulant-substrate interface can be significant even without UV and elevated humidity.
- The optimal EVA gel content range for encapsulant stability is 84-90%, with samples below 70% showing low chemical and optical stability and weak adhesion strength.
- EVA laminates with gel content over 92% are more likely to yellow and show less stable adhesion during aging.
- EVA manufacturer STR recommends a minimum 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination by 180-degree peel to ensure 20-year module lifetime.
- Three degradation mechanisms dominate EVA adhesion loss sequentially: deacetylation initially, then beta-scission, followed by hydrolytic depolymerization.
- Glass/EVA interface experiences more degradation than EVA/cell, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes preferential delamination site.
- Results encourage the PV industry to reduce VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp heat aging.
- Tensile strength, thermal stability, and crosslinking degree were greater for PEVA with lower VA content (18% and 24%) and diminished for higher VA content (33% and 40%) after DH aging.
- PEVA with 18% VA was discouraged as encapsulant due to inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after 2000 hours of DH aging.
- Peel adhesion of self-adhesive tapes on construction surfaces was tested before and after artificial aging to evaluate durability on different building envelope materials.

For each named study, output ONE line in this format:
STUDY: <first_author_last_name> | <year_if_known> | <short_description>

Examples:
STUDY: Trepanowski | 2017 | 12-month ADF vs daily calorie restriction RCT
STUDY: Varady | 2022 | meta-analysis of alternate-day fasting protocols
STUDY: Longo | 2016 | fasting-mimicking diet clinical trial

Output ONLY the STUDY lines. If no named studies are found, output: NONE
